narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40% chance that you would fail it. This threshold is a key part of our admission process, as we aim to select students with the strongest projected outcomes based on patterns from past bar takers. The model's decision hinged on a few key aspects of your profile, starting with your LSAT score, which stood out positively at 40—above the average of about 37 for those who passed the bar. This strong performance pulled the prediction toward success, showing the model viewed it as a solid foundation for your potential. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 was the biggest counterforce, pushing strongly toward a failure prediction since it falls well below the average of nearly 6 for bar passers, signaling potential challenges in sustaining academic momentum. Your upper-middle family income also nudged things in a favorable direction, aligning with the most common bracket among successful students at 45%. Compared to typical admitted applicants—who often have higher decile rankings in later years alongside balanced test scores—your profile showed promise in testing and background but was offset by that lower Year 3 standing. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 trailed the 3.24 average for passers and your full-time enrollment plans matched the norm, they played smaller roles here. Overall, these factors combined to tip the scales just enough against admission. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your later-year grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps through targeted study habits or academic support. Retaking the LSAT isn't urgent given your score, but building a narrative of upward academic trajectory in any additional undergrad or post-bac work could help. Consider programs with bridge courses or bar prep emphasis, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a full-time student from an upper-middle background. With targeted improvements, your LSAT strength positions you well for another shot at success.
